---
title: "St. Andrew's Cross (Hypericum hypericoides)"
slug: "hypericum-hypericoides"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622656000/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "St. Andrew's Cross"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Malpighiales"
tax_family: "Hypericaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Malpighiales", "Hypericaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622656000/medium.jpg" alt="St. Andrew's Cross" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/marykeim">
marykeim</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341973146">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Malpighiales
-   **Family:** Hypericaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=120009)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in dry to mesic habitats.

## Natural history notes

A shrub characterized by its opposite leaves and bright yellow flowers with four petals arranged in a cross-like shape. In Florida, it is commonly found in dry to mesic habitats such as flatwoods, sandhills, and open woodlands. It typically blooms during the warmer months and provides a nectar source for various pollinators.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/120009-Hypericum-hypericoides)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-hypericum-hypericoides/)
